name: Coyote
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Coyote offers access to agate and jasper deposits in the Rio Arriba highlands. Flat terrain at 6741 ft elevation makes this an excellent beginner-friendly site.
description: The Coyote area of Rio Arriba County sits on productive BLM land known for colorful agate and jasper specimens. This location is part of the Española Basin, a geologically rich region where volcanic and silica-bearing formations have concentrated semi-precious stones in accessible deposits. The flat terrain and moderate elevation make it an ideal destination for families and newer rockhounds looking to find quality material without extreme climbing or scrambling. Collectors can explore washes and hillsides to uncover banded agates, jasper nodules, and chalcedony pieces, many suitable for lapidary work or display. Because this is public BLM land with no permit requirement, you can collect year-round within the daily 25-pound personal use limit.
history: This region lies within the Rio Arriba County mineralized zone, where Tertiary-age volcanic activity and hydrothermal alteration deposited silica-rich solutions that solidified into agate and jasper formations. The flat basalt flows and alluvial washes in the Coyote area have historically been worked by local rockhounders and continue to yield quality specimens.
tips: Focus your search in dry washes and arroyos where erosion naturally concentrates heavier stones; look for the banding patterns typical of Rio Arriba agates, often featuring browns, whites, and rusty tones. Use a 5-gallon bucket and hand tools like a pry bar or chisel to carefully extract specimens from the surrounding host rock without damaging them. Early morning collection is recommended to avoid afternoon heat and to spot specimens more easily in angled sunlight. Check recent washouts and erosion scars after seasonal rainfall, as new material is constantly being exposed.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6741 feet on relatively flat terrain with sparse piñon-juniper scrubland. Unpaved roads lead to informal parking areas and collection zones. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting spots ranges from 0.25 to 1.5 miles. Footing is generally stable with sandy and rocky soil, minimal steep slopes. Vegetation is low and scattered; no dense brush impedes access.
best_season: April - October. Higher elevation (6700+ ft) keeps summer heat manageable. Winter snow and cold can make access difficult; early spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) offer ideal conditions with moderate temperatures and lower precipitation.
written_directions: From Coyote town center, head north on NM 96 for approximately 3 miles. Look for informal BLM access roads and pullouts marked by cairns or previous rockhound activity on the west side of the highway. Turn onto any clearly used dirt road and follow it to parking areas near productive wash systems. GPS coordinates (36.165723, -106.616231) mark the general collecting zone; use these as a navigation target.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt roads, washboard surface typical, high-clearance vehicle recommended but passenger cars can access with caution during dry conditions. Avoid after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas scattered along BLM access roads. No designated parking lot; capacity limited to what informal pullouts accommodate. Park clear of road and drainages.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy machinery. Hand tools and small-scale picking only. Respect the land; refill holes and remove trash.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Remove all trash
nearest_services: Coyote town (3 miles): limited services. Española (30 miles): gas, food, water, supplies.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain with minimal elevation gain makes this accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved road to parking requires high-clearance vehicle but walking distances to collection areas are short (under 1.5 miles). Loose rocks and uneven ground require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to remote collecting zones.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Coyote, New Mexico?
faq.answer: The Coyote BLM area is best known for agate and jasper, particularly banded agates with brown, white, and rust coloring. You will also find chalcedony and quartz specimens. Most material is suitable for lapidary work, cabochons, or display pieces.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Coyote on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required to rockhound on this BLM land. You can collect for personal use at no cost under FLPMA rules, as long as you follow the 25-pound daily limit and use only hand tools.

faq.question: How far is Coyote rockhounding site from the nearest town with services?
faq.answer: Coyote town is about 3 miles away with very limited services. Española, the nearest full-service town with gas, food, lodging, and supplies, is approximately 30 miles south.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Coyote beginner-friendly and suitable for families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Coyote is an excellent beginner and family destination. Flat terrain, easy access, and productive agate and jasper deposits make it ideal for children ages 6 and up. The 25-pound daily limit prevents overexertion, and nearby parking means short walks to collecting areas.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound in the Coyote, New Mexico area?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal. The 6741-foot elevation keeps summer heat moderate. Early spring (April-May) and fall (September-October) offer the most comfortable temperatures. Winter snow and cold can make access difficult; summer afternoon heat is manageable at this elevation.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring for rockhounding at Coyote?
faq.answer: Bring hand tools such as a pry bar, chisel, and small hammer. A 5-gallon bucket or collection bag is essential for carrying specimens up to 25 pounds. Wear sturdy footwear due to loose rocks and uneven ground. Bring sunscreen, water, a hat, and a field guide to help identify specimens.

meta_title: Coyote Rockhounding - Free BLM Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony on free BLM land near Coyote, NM. No permit required. Beginner-friendly collecting. Learn access, rules, and tips.
